name: Zermatt
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Display Serif. Its emphasis on sharp tension, precision and editorial restraint aligns it firmly with high-contrast display serifs rather than text-optimised styles.
summary: A sharp, high-contrast display serif by Muhittin Güneş, built for editorial work that favours precision and tactile restraint over decorative excess.
description: Zermatt runs across 14 cuts — seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic — and is shaped by what the designer describes as the point where molten metal meets a sharp edge. Its curves are deliberate rather than expressive, kept tight to serve as a neutral but distinctive base for editorial and typographic messages. Released in 2019 and carrying 512 glyphs, it is positioned for high-end contexts where texture and discipline matter more than personality.
foundry_blurb: Güneş Muhittin describe Zermatt as frozen, surgical and monolithic — a serif defined by precision, restraint and editorial discipline. They frame it as an honest search for form, balancing liquid shapes against sharp tension, and built for those who value a tactile feel and resist unnecessary ornament.

name: Ranua
classification_notes: Classified as Sans Serif by the foundry, with a display orientation driven by deep ink traps and cuts designed for large-size clarity rather than continuous text use.
summary: A display sans by Muhittin Güneş built around deep ink traps and precise cuts that maintain clarity at large sizes.
description: Across 18 cuts spanning Fine to Black with matching italics, Ranua uses deliberate incisions in its forms to prevent strokes from closing up in dense or oversized settings. The result sits between neutral and expressive: structured enough for bold editorial compositions, distinctive enough to carry its own quiet presence on posters and screens. Released in March 2019 and updated to version 003 in early 2026, it covers 761 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Güneş Muhittin describe Ranua as a display typeface shaped by deep ink traps and a focus on clarity at large sizes. Its forms are simple and direct, opened through subtle cuts that prevent the structure from closing in dense or oversized settings. The rhythm, they say, comes from within the cuts, giving it a quiet presence that holds up across posters, screens and bold compositions.

name: Antra
classification_notes: Explicitly classified as Neo-Grotesk by the designer, drawing from the mid-century tradition of Akzidenz-Grotesk with horizontal terminals and restrained contrast typical of rational grotesque designs.
summary: A neo-grotesque by Muhittin Güneş with horizontal terminals and measured contrast, positioned to work across both headline and text roles in design systems.
description: Antra takes Akzidenz-Grotesk as its historical reference point and updates the form for contemporary digital environments. Horizontal terminals and carefully controlled contrast give it a structural reliability that allows it to operate quietly at text sizes or assertively at display scale. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, totalling 18 cuts.
foundry_blurb: Güneş Muhittin describes Antra as rigid, concrete and rational, a contemporary neo-grotesk that evolves the mid-century spirit of Akzidenz-Grotesk for modern systems. The typeface is characterised by its horizontal terminals and proportions, allowing it to function as a confident headline or a quiet supporting voice in complex design environments, with a focus on clarity, rhythm and structural reliability.

name: Gronland
classification_notes: Gronland is described as rooted in digital grid functionalism with a plain proportional skeleton, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition where structure takes precedence over humanist warmth or grotesque character.
summary: A grid-derived geometric sans in 18 cuts, designed by Muhittin Güneş for industrial clarity and long-term neutral utility.
description: Built on a strict digital grid, Gronland strips form back to its structural minimum, offering nine weights from Thin to Black each with a matching italic. Released in 2021 and updated to version 003 in March 2025, it ships as a high-precision variable font covering 761 glyphs. The design positions itself as a plain, unyielding workhorse for contexts where the content, not the typeface, is meant to carry attention.
foundry_blurb: Güneş Muhittin describes Gronland as plain, cold and primary, a proportional sans rooted in strict digital grid functionalism. Built for consistency and long-term use, it is offered as a high-precision variable font that reduces letterform to bare structural truth, providing an industrial base designed to let content stand on its own.

name: Bugrino
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Experimental Sans; it is purpose-built for display use and derives contrast from outline and structure rather than stroke modulation, placing it firmly outside conventional text sans territory.
summary: An experimental display sans by Muhittin Güneş that derives contrast from structural tension rather than stroke weight, mixing rounded forms with unexpected sharp edges across nine weights.
description: Released in August 2022 and updated through 2025, Bugrino spans 18 cuts across nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, totalling 756 glyphs. Contrast comes from the outline and overall structure rather than from variation in stroke weight, and small shifts in proportion give the type an irregular rhythm that stays controlled enough for display work. The design courts visual friction rather than neutrality, making it most useful in contexts where formal unease is an intentional part of the message.
foundry_blurb: Güneş Muhittin describes Bugrino as erratic, friction-driven and unapologetic — an experimental sans shaped by formal tension and unconventional geometry. They note that it generates contrast through outline and structure rather than stroke weight, combining rounded forms with sharp and unexpected edges. Small proportional shifts create a rhythm that feels distinct yet carefully controlled for display use, while the typeface demands engagement through visual resistance, allowing rigid structure and subtle irregularity to coexist.

name: Rupta
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Experimental Sans; it operates as a grotesque with deliberately fractured surface detail, sitting between systematic grid logic and deconstructivist disruption.
summary: A deconstructivist grotesque by Muhittin Güneş that applies controlled fracture to letterforms without surrendering structural legibility.
description: Sixteen cuts span Thin to Black across upright and italic styles, giving the family broad weight coverage for display and editorial use. The design concept frames each letter as a form under pressure, where surface ruptures are introduced within a precise underlying structure rather than at random. The result sits somewhere between a straight grotesque and a more overtly expressive display face.
foundry_blurb: Güneş Muhittin describe Rupta as a deconstructivist grotesque built on the boundary between systematic logic and deliberate disruption, exploring controlled breakage where structures are pushed until they fracture without collapsing. They position it as neither decorative nor chaotic, balancing stability and failure with discipline and intent, with each letter proving that form can remain structurally sound even as its surface begins to rupture.
